clips.viral_hook: prediction markets really represent the purest form of risk out there because
clips.transcript: prediction markets really represent the purest form of risk out there because as you point out Cathy you can go directly to the underlying discrete event that then moves the on an equity And so I actually think of these tools as almost the inverse of derivatives right It's the underlying catalyst that will drive the performance of a stock up or down And so whereas you may want to hedge out some of that risk using a put call spread or many other forms of hedging now you have direct access to those underlying events business KPIs macroeconomic events where you don't need to take on the extra risk that is associated with the market right If you don't want to be exposed to what is happening in Iran well you don't need to take on that exposure through derivatives which are going to inherently have some of that in it You can go directly to the underlying event So I think it's a really amazing opportunity for the financial markets when we've started doing this research And I'll give a sneak preview I think you know in the medium term So over the next few years we believe that this market could explode up to five trillion dollars in notional volume Now right now Cal she is on an annualized run rate to do around one hundred and twenty one hundred and thirty billion dollars in notional volume for this year So we're talking about a massive explosion of where this total market is headed
clips.shorts_title: Why Kathy Wood Thinks Prediction Markets Could Explode to $5 Trillion
clips.description: Nick explains why prediction markets may be the purest way to express risk and why ARK thinks the market could grow massively from here.
clips.selection_reason: Composite 0.77 because it opens with a strong contrarian framing, names concrete market size numbers, and stands alone as a clear thesis clip.

rule_scores.reason: Opens with a bold claim that prediction markets are the purest form of risk.
Frames prediction markets as the inverse of derivatives and a cleaner tool than standard hedges.
This segment is conceptual and does not rely on a visible chart.
A cold viewer can follow the argument without prior setup.

clips.viral_hook: But how high will the dollar get
clips.transcript: But how high will the dollar get Last year the narrative was the U.S. exceptionalism is dead the dollar is in decline and it's inexorable It's going to continue And you can see here many people believe that the dollar will end the year as measured by the DXY at 1036 Now that is very interesting to us because you can see on the next page where we are in the context of history We are just below 99 So what Calixte is suggesting is the people who are willing to trade or make a prediction they actually think the dollar is going to go up And that goes against conventional wisdom Now we agree that it is going to go up mostly because of what I just mentioned about foreign direct investment and our capital-friendly business-friendly tax policies Also deregulation is we think going to increase the return on investment in the United States relative to that in the rest of the world That is dollar positive

clips.viral_hook: In fact a lot of employment indicators suggest we're in a weak environment
clips.transcript: In fact a lot of employment indicators suggest we're in a weak environment And part of it is AI Entry-level jobs are not there for college graduates the way they have been in the past So unit labor costs this is underlying inflation 2.4% What's interesting about that is if you look at the TIPS market that's what the TIPS market says inflation is going to be in other words no real improvement from here If we're right and productivity starts scaling three and into next year four or five percent this unit labor cost growth rate will come down and underlying inflation will come down Here are some other reasons inflation is coming down Again very different from post-COVID when we had that supply shock and many people are saying we're in another supply shock and we are when it comes to oil for sure but we are not in a broad-based supply shock like we were after COVID Nothing like it In fact quite the opposite in housing Housing inflation is nil for existing home prices and still negative for new home prices

clips.viral_hook: can see this is an outright boom
clips.transcript: can see this is an outright boom It's an indicator that I believe that Piper Sandler gave us or produced And you can see it is flat This is U.S. Flatbed Truck Market Demand Index So this is an indicator that something's booming out there And no one's using the word boom but I think there is a call to action The pricing certainly is one reason But I also think there's a lot more foreign direct investment coming into the United States Some of it might be tariff related Some of it is because deregulation and tax policy are so friendly So that's interesting You can see on the services sector both orders and unfortunately prices Prices again I think being impacted by what's going on in the Middle East and it may be opportunistic pricing around that Normally when we see new orders pick up the prices are coming down And I do think that the purple chart will resolve to the downside once the Middle East turmoil clears up to some extent Here is why the pricing is not getting through Sentiment is terrible as measured by the University of Michigan

clips.viral_hook: So here we ask the question will non-farm productivity increase above 3% in any quarter
clips.transcript: So here we ask the question will non-farm productivity increase above 3% in any quarter This is on a year-over-year basis in any quarter in 26 And you can see there's a 42% probability Well we think the probability actually is much higher than that And it's actually for a statistical reason If you look at productivity today you can see we're at roughly 2.5% And you can also see again if you look across this chart this is in the higher half of the history of this chart So pretty strong And as we have said in the know many times we've been in a rolling recession and this is what has happened That's so interesting Housing has been down and out Manufacturing has been down and out Small business down and out Consumers have been in a funk as measured by sentiment And productivity is at 2.5% So we believe that these technologies are already having an impact on productivity And we think in the first quarter when the numbers are reported and they'll be revised two times I think But when the first quarter number is reported if it is up at all on a quarter to quarter basis

clips.viral_hook: Here is another take and I'm going to credit Don Lustwin from Trend Macro for this chart
clips.transcript: Here is another take and I'm going to credit Don Lustwin from Trend Macro for this chart And what he does trying to help people understand the debt and the deficit situation is he says you know when you compare debt to GDP what you're comparing is a balance sheet item to an income statement item the equivalent of Really you should be comparing debt to equity So equity is the black line debt is the purple and GDP is the red And if we go to the next chart you will see that while debt to GDP is above one here the one ratio you can see debt to equity is at 0.5 And actually it's very low by historical standards The other thing I will say is if you look at debt to assets another balance sheet item the asset side of the U.S. balance sheet is really at cost What did the land cost What did the gold cost when it went onto the federal balance sheet Well some of these costs were 50 to 75 years ago
